label: Uneasy
summary: The world nudges up a point to 40, tipping from 'Heavy going' to the bottom of the 'Mixed / uneasy' band as record markets and a run of good science offset a war that will not end. US Central Command carried out a second wave of strikes on Iran aimed at Tehran's ability to menace shipping in the Strait of Hormuz, hitting the island of Greater Tunb and disabling an oil tanker bound for Kharg Island; Iran's health ministry reported more than 260 people injured including women and children, a foreign-ministry spokesman ruled out negotiations, and the US naval blockade of Iranian ports was reinstated. Overnight into July 16 Russian ballistic missiles struck Kyiv, sparking fires in the Sviatoshynskyi and Darnytskyi districts as a Kremlin aide declared Russia's nuclear fleet at 'full combat readiness.' The humanitarian ledger stayed grim: Sudan's war has now killed at least 59,000 and displaced some 13 million with famine spreading; DR Congo's Bundibugyo-Ebola outbreak reached 1,963 cases and more than 700 deaths as exhausted frontline workers threatened to strike; and nine months into the Gaza ceasefire the count of Palestinians killed since it began passed 1,100 with only about 35 percent of promised aid trucks admitted. Europe's deadly summer heat pushed on, with roughly 20,000 excess deaths logged last month and Iberia forecast to hit 43C. Against that, the day's brighter side was real: cooling June inflation, the first monthly US price decline since the pandemic, lifted the S&P 500 to 7,572 and carried Apple to a record high; astronauts took the first medical X-rays in space; and the World Cup final was set for New Jersey with Argentina, who beat England 2-1 on two Messi assists, meeting Spain. The headline matches the rubric formula, held down by an active-war Middle East and an African hunger and disease crisis even as the economic and science columns brighten.

drivers.headline: US Central Command completes a second wave of strikes on Iran aimed at degrading its ability to target vessels in the Strait of Hormuz, hitting the Gulf island of Greater Tunb and disabling an empty oil tanker sailing toward Kharg Island; Iran's health ministry says more than 260 people have been injured in recent strikes, including at least three women and six children, a foreign-ministry spokesman rules out any negotiations with Washington, and the US reinstates its naval blockade of Iranian ports

Traffic through the Strait of Hormuz stays throttled as the US and Iran resume open fighting, with tanker crossings collapsing to a fraction of pre-war levels and Iran moving sanctioned crude in the dark; Brent holds near 86 dollars a barrel after gaining roughly 13 percent over the week, keeping energy and inflation risk elevated across import-dependent economies in Asia and Europe

US stocks climb as data shows June consumer prices fell 0.4 percent, the first monthly decline since the Covid-19 pandemic, easing fears the Federal Reserve will raise rates this month: the S&P 500 finishes up 0.38 percent at 7,572.40, the Nasdaq adds 0.62 percent to 26,269.23 and the Dow gains 150 points to 52,658.64, with Apple touching a new all-time high and Amazon, Alphabet and Microsoft each rising around 3 percent

Russian ballistic missiles strike Kyiv overnight into July 16, with impacts and falling debris sparking fires and damaging non-residential sites in the Sviatoshynskyi and Darnytskyi districts; explosions rock the capital shortly before 1 a.m. as Kremlin aide Nikolai Patrushev tells state media that Russia's nuclear fleet is at 'full combat readiness'

Sudan's war has now killed at least 59,000 people, displaced roughly 13 million and pushed parts of the country into famine, with more than 30 million now needing humanitarian aid; UNICEF reports over 300 children killed or injured in the past six months as fighting in Darfur and Kordofan grinds on

The Democratic Republic of the Congo's Bundibugyo-virus Ebola outbreak reaches 1,963 confirmed cases and more than 700 related deaths, with Ituri province the epicentre and cases confirmed in Uganda; existing Ebola therapies were certified for a different species, and frontline workers threaten to strike over unpaid benefits and missing supplies, raising fears the response could unravel as the virus spreads

Nine months into the US-brokered Gaza ceasefire, the number of Palestinians killed since it began passes 1,110 while only about 35 percent of the promised aid trucks, some 58,664 of 165,000, have entered the territory; aid workers describe Gaza as 'one giant graveyard' as more than 18,000 new cases of chickenpox, skin and parasitic infections are logged in a single week among a population with disrupted health care

Europe's record-breaking summer heat pushes on, with an estimated 20,000 excess deaths across the continent last month and temperature records broken from Spain and Portugal, forecast to hit 43C this week, to France, Germany and the UK, where drought and hosepipe bans spread; scientists say the heat would have been 'virtually impossible' without human-driven climate change

A severe heat wave grips the United States and Canada, with records tied or broken across major cities and a peak of 41C recorded in Atlantic City, New Jersey; the death toll attributed to the North American heat had reached at least 44 by early July as forecasters warn of further deadly surges over already-parched regions

Astronauts take the first medical X-rays in space, a milestone that ends more than four decades in which ultrasound was the only practical imaging tool in orbit; lead researcher Sheyna Gifford of the Mayo Clinic calls a second diagnostic modality 'a dream for aerospace medicine,' opening the way to better diagnosis of illness and injury on long-duration missions

The World Cup final is set for MetLife Stadium in New Jersey on July 19, pitting defending champions Argentina against Spain: Argentina beat England 2-1 in Atlanta on an 85th-minute Enzo Fernandez strike and a stoppage-time Lautaro Martinez header, both assisted by Lionel Messi, while Spain edged France 2-0 in the other semifinal, capping a tournament that has drawn record crowds across North America

The season's positive ledger runs from health to the environment: reporting highlights Europe breathing easier after decades of clean-air policy, the return of swimmers to a cleaned-up River Seine in Paris for a second summer, electric-vehicle sales overtaking petrol cars in Britain for the first time, and England becoming the first European nation to approve a therapy that delays the onset of type 1 diabetes

sources_consulted: Wikipedia (2026 Iran war / 2026 European heatwaves / 2026 North American heat wave / Timeline of the Russo-Ukrainian war / 2026 Ebola epidemic / Weather of 2026)
